value.narrative:
Here's a quick look at the last 90 days of daily tourism occupancy data for Cairns, Gold Coast, Noosa, and Whitsundays. The data covers June 2nd to August 30th, 2024, showing daily occupancy percentages and average daily rates for each region.

Overall, all regions show a general upward trend in occupancy through June and July, followed by some variation in August. Noosa and Whitsundays consistently show higher average daily rates (ADR) than Cairns and Gold Coast, indicating premium pricing or different market segments in those destinations.

Cairns experienced a steady rise in occupancy, peaking in mid-July around 75-80%, before a gradual decline through August. Gold Coast had more pronounced peaks and troughs, with high occupancy around early June, late July, and mid-August, often accompanied by higher ADR spikes. Noosa and Whitsundays both maintained solid occupancy levels through the period, frequently reaching over 70% in July and August, and consistently recorded the highest ADRs across all regions.

No obvious anomalies stand out, but the consistent higher ADR for Noosa and Whitsundays compared to Cairns and Gold Coast is worth keeping an eye on for strategic planning and understanding market positioning. The Gold Coast's more fluctuating occupancy might suggest a market more sensitive to specific events or holidays.

chartSpec.title: Daily Occupancy Percentage by Region (June - August 2024)

value.narrative:
Over the last three months, Queensland's key tourism regions have shown strong performance, particularly during the peak winter holiday period in July. We've observed a general increase in hotel occupancy from June, reaching peaks in mid-July, followed by a slight moderation in August.

Looking at occupancy rates, Cairns, Whitsundays, and Noosa experienced the highest peak occupancies, often surpassing 70% and even reaching over 80% in Cairns during mid-July. The Gold Coast, while still performing well, generally had slightly lower peak occupancy rates compared to these tropical destinations, often staying below 70%.

When it comes to the average daily rate, Noosa and Whitsundays stand out, consistently commanding the highest prices for accommodation, frequently above $350 and peaking well over $400. The Gold Coast also achieved high rates, especially on weekends, sometimes reaching almost $400. Cairns, while having strong occupancy, generally offered more competitive average daily rates, typically between $270 and $310.

chartSpec.description: Daily Hotel Occupancy Trends by Region for June-August 2024.
chartSpec.title: Daily Hotel Occupancy Trends by Region (June-August 2024)

value.narrative:
Looking at the past three months, tourism across Queensland has seen a healthy winter and early spring period. July was a standout month for most regions, showing the highest occupancy rates and strong average daily rates.

When we compare regions, Noosa and Whitsundays consistently command the highest prices, with average daily rates (ADR) often exceeding $350 and even climbing above $400. This suggests a premium market that's willing to pay for these destinations. Cairns also performed well, particularly in mid-July, where it achieved the highest occupancy percentage among all regions, though its ADR tends to hover around the $300 mark. The Gold Coast shows more variability; while its baseline occupancy and ADR can be lower, it experienced significant spikes in both, indicating strong demand during specific events or weekends.

For your pricing decisions, consider the strong market position of Noosa and Whitsundays, which allows for premium rates. In Cairns, focus on maximizing occupancy during strong demand periods and subtly testing higher rates as demand strengthens. For the Gold Coast, identifying and capitalizing on those specific peak demand days is crucial for optimizing your revenue, as these periods allow for significant rate increases. Overall, the data points to a generally robust market from June to August, with July being the peak performing month.
